extra.message: Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as 'qwerty12345' in application configuration. This allows session forgery and cookie tampering if the source code is disclosed.
Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as '55555' in application configuration. This weak, hardcoded secret allows session forgery and cookie tampering.
Decrypted cookie value 'username' is written directly to the HTTP response without HTML escaping, enabling reflected/stored XSS if an attacker controls the cookie plaintext.

User-supplied URL from request.args is validated only with startswith() and then passed to urllib.request.urlopen(). This is trivially bypassed (e.g., https://insecurewebapp.com.evil.com or https://insecurewebapp.com@evil.com) leading to server-side request forgery.

Payment signature is computed as SHA256(secret || data) instead of using HMAC. This construction is vulnerable to length extension attacks, allowing an attacker to forge valid signatures for arbitrary appended data.

cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

extra.message: Payment signature is computed as SHA256(secret || data) instead of using HMAC. This construction is vulnerable to length extension attacks, allowing an attacker to forge valid signatures for arbitrary appended data.

cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

extra.message: User-controlled request path is concatenated to os.getcwd() after a trivial regex check and then opened for reading. The check only matches '/../../' at the start with re.match, so paths like /foo/../../etc/passwd bypass it entirely, allowing arbitrary file read.

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

extra.message: SSL context has check_hostname set to False, disabling hostname verification. This allows man-in-the-middle attacks because the client will accept any certificate regardless of whether it matches the target hostname.

cwe: CWE-297: Improper Validation of Certificate with Host Mismatch
